food_name: Ricotta and Spinach Ravioli with Tomato Sauce
aliases: Stuffed Pasta with Ricotta and Spinach

english: Stuffed Pasta with Ricotta and Spinach

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302114 covers ravioli as a food product category; the specific ricotta-and-spinach filling variant is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers ravioli as a pasta category; the specific filling (ricotta and spinach) and sauce (tomato) are not individually faceted at this level. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 covers pasta dish as a food concept; no specific ravioli ricotta spinach code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of ricotta and spinach ravioli is approximately 350 g, comprising filled pasta pieces, tomato sauce, grated Pecorino, and a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil.

nutrient_source.title: Ravioli, cheese-filled, with tomato sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; supplemented with USDA FDC entry for cheese ravioli with tomato sauce
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central does not carry a specific buffalo-ricotta-and-spinach ravioli entry. Values are adapted from USDA FDC entries for cheese-filled ravioli with tomato sauce (FDC ID 172150 and related entries), adjusted for the spinach-and-ricotta filling profile and olive-oil-dressed tomato sauce. Macros, fibre, sodium, and saturated fat are consistent with published USDA data for this pasta category. Values represent the plated dish as served (pasta + filling + sauce + oil + cheese topping), normalised to 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for fresh pasta / filled pasta
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ricotta-and-spinach ravioli specifically. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values of 39–47 for fresh filled pasta and cheese ravioli (white flour, egg-based dough, boiled). A GI of 39 is used here, representing the lower end of the fresh filled pasta range, consistent with the egg-enriched fresh pasta dough and protein-rich ricotta filling which slow gastric emptying. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (39 × 68.25) / 100 ≈ 26.6, using 350 g serving × 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 68.25 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1085 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of this dish provides approximately 1085 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from the tomato sauce, aged Pecorino cheese, and pasta dough; portion control or requesting reduced-salt preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~26.6 per restaurant serving) despite low GI (39)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of fresh filled pasta is low (approximately 39), the large restaurant portion (~350 g) delivers approximately 68 g of available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~27, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; a half-portion with a side salad is recommended to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ricotta, Pecorino, and egg-enriched pasta dough (~2.9 g per 100 g; ~10.2 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of buffalo ricotta, aged Pecorino, and egg-enriched pasta contributes approximately 10 g of saturated fat per restaurant serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider requesting a lighter cheese topping to reduce saturated fat intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ricotta and Spinach Ravioli (Ravioli Ricotta e Spinaci) is a classic Italian stuffed pasta dish consisting of square or round fresh egg-pasta parcels filled with a mixture of creamy ricotta cheese — in this restaurant version, buffalo ricotta — baby spinach, and fresh herbs. The filled pasta is boiled until tender and served with a light tomato sauce, a generous grating of aged Pecorino Romano cheese, and a drizzle of organic extra virgin olive oil. The texture is soft and yielding with a rich, milky filling that contrasts with the bright acidity of the tomato sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~168 kcal per 100 g), a balanced macronutrient profile with meaningful protein from the dairy filling (~7.2 g per 100 g), and a low glycemic index (~39) attributable to the egg-enriched fresh pasta dough and protein-rich ricotta filling. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan, and contains gluten, eggs, and dairy. Clinically, the sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (~2.9 g per 100 g) are relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the moderate glycemic load of a full restaurant serving (~26.6) warrants portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is a culturally authentic Italian primo piatto (first course) and is widely served in Italian restaurants across Singapore and globally.

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central or other approved-source entry exists for buffalo-ricotta-and-spinach ravioli with tomato sauce as a plated restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC cheese-filled ravioli with tomato sauce entries, which are the closest approved-source analogues. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database range for fresh filled pasta. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient profile is well-characterised and the USDA analogues are nutritionally close.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cheese-filled ravioli with tomato sauce; the specific buffalo-ricotta-and-spinach variant is not individually listed in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FDC source entries for this pasta category. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed as effectively zero for a fresh pasta dish made with ricotta, spinach, and olive oil — no hydrogenated fats are present. 4. GI of 39 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database range for fresh filled pasta (GI 39–47); no direct measurement for this specific dish exists in the approved GI literature. 5. Serving size of 350 g is estimated for a restaurant plate as served at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, Singapore; actual portion may vary. 6. The dish is listed as vegetarian (V) on the field survey menu; dietary flag is_vegetarian is set to true. 7. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Local names in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not applicable and are left null. The romanized Italian name 'Ravioli Ricotta e Spinaci' is preserved in romanized_local and aliases.
